graph_vector_fulltext.message:
Under this Regulation, “real-time” biometric identification systems are distinguished by how quickly the biometric identification happens: the capturing of biometric data, the comparison, and the identification occur “all instantaneously, near-instantaneously or in any event without a significant delay,” and they use “live material” or “near-live material.” The context also notes that these “real-time” systems are particularly intrusive, especially when used as “real-time remote biometric identification” in publicly accessible spaces for law-enforcement purposes.

“Post” (post-remote) biometric identification systems, by contrast, operate on a “closed data set of legally acquired video footage,” rather than live/near-live material. They are described as less impactful on fundamental rights than real-time remote biometric identification, but still intrusive and therefore expected to be “subject to safeguards” and used only in ways that are legitimate, proportionate, and strictly necessary, targeted by person, location, and time. The Regulation additionally stresses that conditions for post-remote biometric identification should not be used to circumvent the prohibition and strict exceptions applicable to real-time remote biometric identification.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: How does this Regulation define and distinguish 'real-time' biometric identification systems from 'post' biometric identification systems?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
Under this Regulation, “real-time” biometric identification systems are distinguished by how quickly the biometric identification happens: the capturing of biometric data, the comparison, and the identification occur “all instantaneously, near-instantaneously or in any event without a significant delay,” and they use “live material” or “near-live material.” The context also notes that these “real-time” systems are particularly intrusive, especially when used as “real-time remote biometric identification” in publicly accessible spaces for law-enforcement purposes.

“Post” (post-remote) biometric identification systems, by contrast, operate on a “closed data set of legally acquired video footage,” rather than live/near-live material. They are described as less impactful on fundamental rights than real-time remote biometric identification, but still intrusive and therefore expected to be “subject to safeguards” and used only in ways that are legitimate, proportionate, and strictly necessary, targeted by person, location, and time. The Regulation additionally stresses that conditions for post-remote biometric identification should not be used to circumvent the prohibition and strict exceptions applicable to real-time remote biometric identification.

  case, post-remote biometric identification systems should not be used in the framework of law enforcement to lead  to indiscriminate surveillance. The conditions for post-remote biometric identification should in any case not  provide a basis to circumvent the conditions of the prohibition and strict exceptions for real time remote biometric  identification. (96) In order to efficiently ensure that fundamental rights are protected, deployers of high-risk AI systems that are bodies  governed by public law, or private entities providing
----
 an annex to this Regulation is based on the 32 criminal  offences listed in the Council Framework Decision 2002/584/JHA (18), taking into account that some of those  offences are, in practice, likely to be more relevant than others, in that the recourse to ‘real-time’ remote biometric  identification could, foreseeably, be necessary and proportionate to highly varying degrees for the practical pursuit  of the localisation or identification of a perpetrator or suspect of the different
----
 justified by the fact that such systems are likely to have a minor impact on fundamental  rights of natural persons compared to the remote biometric identification systems which may be used for the  processing of the biometric data of a large number of persons without their active involvement. In the case of  ‘real-time’ systems, the capturing of the biometric data, the comparison and the identification occur all  instantaneously, near-instantaneously or in any event without a significant delay.

an annex to this Regulation is based on the 32 criminal offences listed in the Council Framework Decision 2002/584/JHA (18), taking into account that some of those offences are, in practice, likely to be more relevant than others, in that the recourse to ‘real-time’ remote biometric identification could, foreseeably, be necessary and proportionate to highly varying degrees for the practical pursuit of the localisation or identification of a perpetrator or suspect of the different

justified by the fact that such systems are likely to have a minor impact on fundamental rights of natural persons compared to the remote biometric identification systems which may be used for the processing of the biometric data of a large number of persons without their active involvement. In the case of ‘real-time’ systems, the capturing of the biometric data, the comparison and the identification occur all instantaneously, near-instantaneously or in any event without a significant delay.
